_**User**_

This is outside the roadmap - but I want you to add it to the roadmap as a phase 3. 
Also I want you to add documentation about all of this task. 


You are a Principal UI Architect, Design System Engineer, UX Researcher, Blazor Architect, and MudBlazor expert.

Your mission is NOT to clone a website.

Your mission is to reverse engineer the design language, UX philosophy, interaction patterns, accessibility principles, and visual identity of the following website and transform them into a complete enterprise-grade Design System and UI Kit for a MudBlazor application.

Target website:

https://www.1030.be/fr

This is a government website. Assume the resulting application will be used internally by municipal employees for complex administrative workflows involving large forms, tables, document management, citizen records, appointments, searches, dashboards, and back-office operations.

The goal is to preserve the visual identity and usability philosophy while creating a reusable, maintainable design system.

Never produce a pixel-perfect clone.

Never copy HTML or CSS directly.

Instead, infer the design decisions behind the interface.

Favor MudBlazor components whenever possible.

Custom CSS should be minimal.

Configuration should always be preferred over styling.

The resulting design system should feel like an official extension of the municipality's digital identity.

------------------------------------------------------------
PROJECT OBJECTIVES
------------------------------------------------------------

Design a complete reusable UI framework that includes:

• Design Tokens
• MudTheme
• Global CSS
• Reusable Components
• Layouts
• Page Templates
• Form Standards
• Data Visualization Standards
• Accessibility Guidelines
• UX Principles
• Naming Conventions
• Component Documentation

The final result should be suitable for an application with hundreds of pages.

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 1 — RESEARCH
------------------------------------------------------------

Explore enough of the website to understand:

• Home page
• News pages
• Administrative pages
• Contact pages
• Forms
• Navigation
• Footer
• Search
• Mobile layout
• Responsive behavior
• Error pages
• PDF pages if relevant
• Service pages

Document recurring visual patterns.

Do not stop after inspecting a single page.

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 2 — VISUAL IDENTITY
------------------------------------------------------------

Extract:

Primary colors

Secondary colors

Accent colors

Neutral palette

Semantic colors

Success

Warning

Error

Info

Background colors

Surface colors

Border colors

Text colors

Hover colors

Disabled colors

Focus colors

For every color provide:

HEX

RGB

HSL

Suggested MudBlazor Palette property

Usage

Contrast ratio

WCAG compliance

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 3 — TYPOGRAPHY
------------------------------------------------------------

Identify:

Font families

Fallback fonts

Weights

Sizes

Line heights

Letter spacing

Paragraph spacing

Heading hierarchy

Body hierarchy

Button typography

Table typography

Navigation typography

Produce a complete typography scale.

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 4 — SPACING SYSTEM
------------------------------------------------------------

Determine the spacing scale.

Example:

2

4

8

12

16

20

24

32

40

48

64

80

96

Define:

Page padding

Container widths

Section spacing

Card spacing

Dialog spacing

Form spacing

Table spacing

Toolbar spacing

Grid spacing

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 5 — SHADOWS
------------------------------------------------------------

Infer the elevation system.

Produce a reusable elevation scale.

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 6 — BORDER SYSTEM
------------------------------------------------------------

Determine:

Border radius

Border thickness

Input radius

Card radius

Dialog radius

Chip radius

Button radius

Table radius

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 7 — ICONOGRAPHY
------------------------------------------------------------

Determine:

Preferred icon family

Sizes

Stroke widths

Filled vs outlined usage

When icons are used

When text is preferred

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 8 — MOTION
------------------------------------------------------------

Infer:

Animation duration

Hover effects

Transitions

Focus animations

Dialog animations

Drawer animations

Loading animations

Snackbar behavior

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 9 — RESPONSIVE DESIGN
------------------------------------------------------------

Determine:

Breakpoints

Desktop

Laptop

Tablet

Mobile

Drawer behavior

Navigation collapse

Typography scaling

Responsive spacing

Responsive grids

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 10 — ACCESSIBILITY
------------------------------------------------------------

Evaluate:

Keyboard navigation

Tab order

ARIA usage

Contrast

Focus indicators

Error presentation

Validation messages

Color dependence

Screen reader friendliness

WCAG compliance

Suggest improvements where appropriate.

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 11 — UX PRINCIPLES
------------------------------------------------------------

Infer the design philosophy.

Explain observations such as:

Minimalism

Government identity

Readability

Trust

Professionalism

Consistency

Low cognitive load

Accessibility-first

Large hit targets

Simple navigation

Explain WHY each decision appears intentional.

------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 15 — MUDTHEME
------------------------------------------------------------

Generate a production-ready MudTheme.

Include:

PaletteLight

PaletteDark (if appropriate)

Typography

LayoutProperties

Breakpoints

Shadows

ZIndex

PseudoCss

Everything should compile without modification.

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 16 — GLOBAL CSS
------------------------------------------------------------

Generate a minimal app.css.

Only include rules that cannot be expressed through MudTheme.

Explain every custom rule.

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 17 — BUILD A COMPLETE UI KIT
------------------------------------------------------------

Design reusable wrapper components.

Never expose raw MudBlazor directly to application pages unless justified.

Examples:

<AppIconButton>

<AppCard>

<AppPanel>

<AppSaveCancelBar>

<AppPageTitle>

<AppContentContainer>

<AppActionBar>

<AppFormSection>

<AppFieldLabel>

<AppInfoBox>

<AppQuickActions>

For every wrapper specify:

Public API

Parameters

Composition

Underlying MudBlazor components

Accessibility considerations

When to use

When not to use

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 18 — PAGE TEMPLATES
------------------------------------------------------------

Create reusable layouts for:

Dashboard

List Page

Details Page

Edit Form

Wizard

Document Viewer

Citizen Profile

Appointment Management

Administration Page

Settings Page

Login

Error Page

Empty Page

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 19 — ARCHITECTURE
------------------------------------------------------------

Recommend the folder structure.

Example:

DesignSystem/

Theme/

Tokens/

Layouts/

Components/

Forms/

Navigation/

Dialogs/

Tables/

Cards/

Icons/

Styles/

Templates/

Utilities/

Document how developers should build new pages.

------------------------------------------------------------
STEP 20 — DOCUMENTATION
------------------------------------------------------------

Produce a concise Design System Guide explaining:

Visual principles

Spacing rules

Typography rules

Color usage

Component selection

Accessibility

Do's and Don'ts

Naming conventions

Code conventions

------------------------------------------------------------
FINAL DELIVERABLE
------------------------------------------------------------

Produce the deliverables in this exact order:

1. Executive Summary

2. UX Philosophy

3. Visual Identity Analysis

4. Design Tokens

5. Typography

6. Color Palette

7. Spacing System

8. Elevation System

9. Border System

10. Motion System

11. Accessibility Review

12. Component Catalogue

13. MudBlazor Mapping

14. MudTheme.cs

15. app.css

16. Wrapper Component Specifications

17. Page Templates

18. Folder Structure

19. Developer Guidelines

20. Future Recommendations

The design system should be production-ready, scalable to hundreds of pages, optimized for maintainability, and suitable for long-term use in a municipal information system built with Blazor, MudBlazor, Minimal APIs, Vertical Slice Architecture, FluentValidation, and Domain-Driven Design.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'm mapping out the design system with a deep institutional blue for primary elements, turquoise accents for focus states and interactive components, and a dark red for error states with a light pink background.

Typography uses Open Sans throughout with a clear hierarchy—headings from 2rem down to smaller sizes, body text at 1rem with 1.5 line height, and buttons at 1.25rem bold for accessibility. Buttons are square with no radius, a minimum 48px height for touch targets, and use shadow on hover with reduced opacity when active.

The palette includes accent colors from the calendar (yellow, green, orange, red-pink), neutral grays for text and borders, and subtle shadows throughout. Motion uses a 0.5s transition as the default with faster 0.2-0.3s easing for interactions, and the breakpoints span from 576px up to 1200px.

The container maxes out at 1280-1320px, with a universal 3px teal focus outline for accessibility. The layout structure uses a header, breadcrumb nav, main content area, and footer with a dark blue wrapper, plus zebra striping on tables and square turquoise tags. I need to compute contrast ratios to verify accessibility compliance, then organize the documentation into focused files covering design tokens, color palette, and the actual C# theme code that compiles without modification.

For the file structure, I'm planning eight core documents under `docs/design-system/`: a README with the index and UX philosophy, a visual identity guide covering research and design specs, accessibility guidelines, a component catalogue mapping to MudBlazor, the theme and CSS code, UI kit specs, page templates, and architecture documentation. I'm also considering how to handle the Phase 3 roadmap document—the user asked to add it as a phase 3 item, but there's already a phase 3 in the roadmap, and the phases folder is meant for completed phases, not planned ones.

The real issue is that the existing roadmap has phases 0–10 with phase 3 already assigned to Address & household. Since the user explicitly said "add it to the roadmap as a phase 3," I need to insert the design system as the new Phase 3 and renumber everything after it—so Address & household shifts to Phase 4, and so on through Phase 11. I'll update the phases README table to reflect this. I also need to calculate contrast ratios for the color palette to ensure WCAG compliance.I've identified the site's design system: Open Sans typography, deep blue (#00537F), turquoise focus outlines (#08B0A0), 48px square buttons, subtle shadows, zebra tables, and a blue footer. Now I'll calculate the contrast ratios to verify WCAG compliance.
</details></think>

```bash
python3 - <<'EOF'
def srgb(c):
    c=c/255
    return c/12.92 if c<=0.03928 else ((c+0.055)/1.055)**2.4
def lum(hexc):
    hexc=hexc.lstrip('#')
    r,g,b=(int(hexc[i:i+2],16) for i in (0,2,4))
    return 0.2126*srgb(r)+0.7152*srgb(g)+0.0722*srgb(b)
def ratio(a,b):
    la,lb=lum(a),lum(b)
    if la<lb: la,lb=lb,la
    return (la+0.05)/(lb+0.05)
pairs=[("00537F","FFFFFF"),("FFFFFF","00537F"),("08B0A0","FFFFFF"),("111111","FFFFFF"),("202020","FFFFFF"),("444444","FFFFFF"),("707070","FFFFFF"),("9A0D20","FFFFFF"),("9A0D20","F9DCE5"),("FDC300","111111"),("A2C73A","111111"),("EE743B","FFFFFF"),("EE743B","111111"),("08B0A0","111111"),("00537F","F3F3F3"),("FFFFFF","1B4F72"),("111111","F3F3F3"),("2E7D32","FFFFFF"),("B75500","FFFFFF"),("0B6BA8","FFFFFF"),("CE7D88","111111")]
for a,b in pairs:
    print(f"#{a} on #{b}: {ratio(a,b):.2f}")
def hsl(hexc):
    hexc=hexc.lstrip('#')
    r,g,b=(int(hexc[i:i+2],16)/255 for i in (0,2,4))
    mx,mn=max(r,g,b),min(r,g,b)
    l=(mx+mn)/2
    if mx==mn: h=s=0
    else:
        d=mx-mn
        s=d/(2-mx-mn) if l>0.5 else d/(mx+mn)
        if mx==r: h=((g-b)/d+(6 if g<b else 0))
        elif mx==g: h=(b-r)/d+2
        else: h=(r-g)/d+4
        h/=6
    return f"hsl({round(h*360)}, {round(s*100)}%, {round(l*100)}%)"
print("---HSL---")
for c in ["00537F","08B0A0","FDC300","A2C73A","EE743B","9A0D20","F9DCE5","111111","202020","444444","707070","AEAEAE","DADADA","ECECEC","F0F0F0","F3F3F3","E1E4F4","CE7D88"]:
    r,g,b=(int(c[i:i+2],16) for i in (0,2,4))
    print(f"#{c}: rgb({r},{g},{b}) {hsl(c)}")
EOF
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I've gathered the key visual identity details for 1030.be: the primary color is a deep Schaerbeek blue with strong AAA contrast, there's a turquoise accent used for all focus states, and a palette of secondary brand colors including yellow, green, orange, and rose.

The typography uses Open Sans throughout with specific sizing for headings and body text, buttons are square with bold weight and a 48px minimum height for accessibility, and the design system favors sharp corners (no border radius) for brand components while using subtle shadows and a 0.5-second transition timing for motion effects.

For layout, I'm working with breakpoints at 576, 640, 768, 992, 1024, and 1200 pixels, a maximum container width of 1280–1320px, and a footer styled in solid blue with a five-column grid. Tables get strong bottom borders under headers, zebra striping on odd rows, and generous padding, while accessibility is built in with a teal focus outline, skip links, and aria-labels throughout.

Now for the deliverables—I need to update the roadmap. The user wants this added as Phase 3, but there's already a Phase 3 (Address & household) in the existing roadmap that goes through Phase 10. Since they explicitly said "phase 3," I think the right move is to insert a new Phase 3 for the design system and renumber everything after it, shifting the current Phase 3–10 to become Phase 4–11.

That renumbering cascades into other docs though. TECH-STACK.md and the phases README both reference phase numbers, so I'll need to search for and update those cross-references too. Then I'll create documentation for the design system itself—probably a folder structure under `docs/design-system/` with separate files for visual identity, components, and patterns rather than one monolithic document.

For the MudTheme code, I'm checking what version of MudBlazor we're targeting and then writing out the complete theme configuration...

Writing theme configuration...I've documented 1030.be's design system. Now I'll check the MudBlazor version and scan for phase-number references that need updating if we insert a new Phase 3.

Typography is consistently Open Sans with a 1rem base and specific scaling for headings and buttons.

For spacing, they use a rem-based scale (0.25 through 3rem) that maps to an 8px grid, and their layout maxes out at 1280–1320px. The design language favors sharp, rectangular forms with minimal shadows and slow transitions (0.5s for links/buttons, 0.2–0.3s for micro-interactions). The navigation includes breadcrumbs with an underline-grow animation, a mega menu, and accessibility features like skip links and language switching (French/Dutch with an easy language option). The Palette uses MudColor types that accept string conversions, Shadows has an Elevation array with 26 entries, ZIndex defines elevation levels for drawers, popovers, app bars, dialogs, snackbars and tooltips, and there's also PseudoCss and LayoutProperties for border radius and drawer dimensions. _**Agent (claude-fable-5)**_

Done. The design system for 1030.be is now specified as **Roadmap Phase 3** with a complete documentation set under `docs/design-system/`. Here's what happened and what I found.

## Research

I fetched the live site (home, procedures, news, contact/opening hours, search, 404) plus its compiled stylesheet, and extracted the actual values rather than guessing: institutional blue `#00537F` (the dominant color, 42 uses), yellow accent `#FDC300`, a global 3px turquoise `#08B0A0` focus outline on *every* element, error red `#9A0D20` on pink `#F9DCE5`, Open Sans 400/600/700 only, a 32/24/20/16px bold type scale, square buttons with 48px min-height, two soft ambient shadows, and a 1280px container. I also computed WCAG contrast ratios for every pair — which surfaced that the site's turquoise (2.72:1) and orange (2.92:1) fail AA as text, so the system keeps them for non-text roles and defines darkened AA variants for text.

## Roadmap changes

- **New Phase 3 — Design system & UI kit** inserted in `docs/ROADMAP.md`; former Phases 3–10 renumbered to 4–11. I fixed all cross-references to the old numbering in `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `TESTING.md`, `TECH-STACK.md`, both ADRs, `phases/README.md`, and the Phase 1 delivery notes. (The `DOMAIN.md` phase table refers to IDEA phases, so it was left alone.)
- **`docs/phases/phase-3-design-system.md`** — the delivery plan: five slices (theme+tokens → core wrappers → data wrappers → forms/dialogs → retrofit of Phase 1 pages), tests (bUnit, token contrast assertions), demo script, and risks.

## Design system documentation (`docs/design-system/`)

Eight documents covering all deliverables: `README.md` (executive summary, UX philosophy, visual identity analysis with the deliberate deviations table), `01-design-tokens.md` (full palette with HEX/RGB/HSL/contrast/WCAG per color, type scale, spacing, elevation, borders, icons, motion, breakpoints), `02-accessibility.md` (what the site does well, what it gets wrong, and enforceable rules), `03-component-catalogue.md` (inventory mapped to MudBlazor with standard parameters and rationale), `04-mudtheme-and-css.md`, `05-ui-kit.md` (~30 `App*` wrapper specs with API, composition, and when-not-to-use), `06-page-templates.md` (13 templates), and `07-architecture-and-guidelines.md` (folder structure, naming, do's/don'ts, governance, future recommendations).

Two things worth flagging:

- The `SchaerbeekTheme.cs` and token classes in doc 04 are **verified to compile against MudBlazor 8.15.0** — I built them in a throwaway .NET 10 project (one fix needed: `PseudoCss.Scrollbar` doesn't exist in 8.15).
- No application code was changed — the current `MainLayout` inline theme and `app.css` stay as-is until Phase 3 is actually implemented; the docs specify their replacements.
